Provenance/Significance: This clear #572 BMX number plate was used by local rider John Stancliff during organized BMX competition. Clear number plates became popular among some racers for their distinctive appearance, allowing bike graphics and frame details to remain visible through the plate. However, because transparent plates could make rider numbers harder for officials to read during races, sanctioning bodies such as the National Bicycle League later prohibited their use in competition. Artifacts like this plate illustrate a period of experimentation in BMX equipment before stricter race regulations were adopted.
Additional information: The National Bicycle League (NBL) was one of the primary sanctioning bodies for BMX racing in the United States from the 1970s until its merger with USA BMX in 2011. During its operation, the NBL periodically updated equipment rules to ensure rider identification and safety during competition.

Item Name: Greg Mathias Signed Team USA Jersey
Provenance/Significance: This Team USA jersey signed by Greg Mathias features a design inspired by the classic JMC Racing style associated with Mathias during his BMX racing career. Additional information:
"USA BMX is stoked to reveal this year's Team USA jersey, which is a direct nod to the patriotic look of an iconic American BMX brand - JMC Racing ... specifically, with a salute to the stylish BMX Hall of Famer from Oregon, Darrell Young.

These jerseys will be worn by American racers attending the UCI World Championships in Nantes, France - held during July 26-31, 2022. Traditionally, at the end of each Worlds, attendees trade their jerseys with fellow competitors from other countries - and U.S. jerseys are always a much sought-after item. 

For those curious on their BMX history, research Team USA's classic look by googling JMC and Darrell Young. All of us hope to see this year's squad channel their inner-DY, and not only be as fast as him, but just as smooth."

Item Name: DIRTWERX 24" cruiser prototype frame and fork
Provenance/Significance: This DIRTWERX 24-inch cruiser prototype frame and fork represents an experimental BMX racing design developed during a period when builders were exploring new frame geometries and materials for cruiser-class racing. Prototype frames such as this were often produced in limited numbers to test performance and handling before potential production. Artifacts like this illustrate the innovation and experimentation that have long driven BMX frame design and development.
Additional information: DIRTWERX was founded by BMX pioneer Harry Leary in 1996 after the collapse of the BMX company Balance, where Leary had served as general manager. Wanting to build something of his own after decades of racing and promoting other brands, Leary launched DIRTWERX as a race-driven BMX company, focusing on high-quality chromoly frames, forks, and handlebars. The brand emphasized durability and ride characteristics over lightweight aluminum construction, reflecting Leary’s belief that steel provided better performance and longevity for most riders. Though a small operation, DIRTWERX products were exported internationally and developed through Leary’s continued involvement in BMX racing and rider feedback.

Provenance/Significance: This DIRTWERX “PR12” prototype frame was owned by BMX pioneer Harry Leary and represents an experimental design developed during his later involvement with DIRTWERX frame projects. Prototype frames such as this were typically produced in limited numbers to test geometry, materials, or performance before potential production. Artifacts like the PR12 highlight the continued innovation and hands-on frame development that Leary remained involved with throughout his BMX career.
Additional information: DIRTWERX was founded by BMX pioneer Harry Leary in 1996 after the collapse of the BMX company Balance, where Leary had served as general manager. Wanting to build something of his own after decades of racing and promoting other brands, Leary launched DIRTWERX as a race-driven BMX company, focusing on high-quality chromoly frames, forks, and handlebars. The brand emphasized durability and ride characteristics over lightweight aluminum construction, reflecting Leary’s belief that steel provided better performance and longevity for most riders. Though a small operation, DIRTWERX products were exported internationally and developed through Leary’s continued involvement in BMX racing and rider feedback.

Item Name:
The F-1 Challenge Trophy
Title trophy for the 20" BMX sport of F1/F-1 racing. 1986
Provenance/Significance: This F-1 Challenge Trophy (1986) represents a championship title in the 20-inch BMX discipline known as F-1 racing, a high-level professional racing format that emerged during the 1980s BMX boom. F-1 events brought together top riders competing for prestige and prize money in a format inspired by the structure and branding of Formula 1 motorsport. Trophies like this reflect the efforts during the mid-1980s to elevate BMX racing into a more professional and widely promoted competitive sport.
Additional information: F-1 BMX racing referred to a short-lived mid-1980s experiment with 20-inch geared BMX race bikes, originally promoted with drivetrain technology developed by Shimano. Unlike traditional single-speed BMX bikes, F-1 bikes used multi-speed gearing systems designed to increase speed on larger tracks. Several manufacturers—including Haro Bikes and Diamondback Bicycles—produced F-1 style race bikes, though the concept ultimately remained a niche format within BMX racing.

Provenance/Significance: These Diamondback Harry Leary Turbo BMX forks marking “HLT3” stamped on the dropout. The included baseball reflects the Japanese manufacturing origin of the forks, a period when many high-quality BMX components were produced in Japan before later production shifted to Taiwan.
Additional information: During the early 1980s, Diamondback Bicycles produced many of its high-end BMX components in Japan, where precision manufacturing and high-quality chromoly fabrication were widely respected in the cycling industry. As BMX production expanded later in the decade, manufacturing gradually shifted to Taiwan, where growing bicycle factories allowed companies to produce frames and components at larger scale while maintaining competitive pricing for the rapidly growing BMX market.

Item Name: Harry Leary Fall Risk Racing 2023 Numberplate decal
Provenance/Significance: This Fall Risk Racing #5 number plate decal from 2023 represents BMX legend Harry Leary during his continued participation in BMX racing later in life. The number 5 identifies Leary’s race number within the veteran racing class, while the “Fall Risk Racing” name reflects the humor and camaraderie shared among longtime BMX racers who remain active in the sport decades after its early growth.
Additional information: Fall Risk Racing is a BMX racing team formed by veteran riders who continued competing later in life, often embracing the humor of aging racers still active in the sport. Riders such as Harry Leary participated in events under the Fall Risk Racing banner, reflecting the strong camaraderie among longtime BMX competitors.

Item Name: Linda and Harry Leary Signed /Copy - The museum owns the jersey and frame from this picture.
Provenance/Significance: A unique historical tribute to BMX legend Harry Leary. This item is a certified copy of the only signature Harry’s wife, Linda Leary, has of him. This specific copy was signed and gifted by Linda to the Lititz BMX collection. Note: The museum also houses the original Factory Jersey and Frame featured in this iconic photograph.
Additional information: This signed photo of Harry Leary and Linda Leary documents a moment connected to artifacts preserved in the Lititz BMX Archive. The museum also holds the Honda jersey and Specialized Hemi frame shown in the image, creating a direct visual link between the photograph and the physical items in the collection.

Provenance/Significance: This is the last HLT 4 frame owned and ridden by Harry Leary. According to Harry, this specific bike was accidentally run over by a vehicle with a bunch of kids while he was setting up for a series of Sprints. While some in the community remain skeptical of the "hit-and-run" account, the frame stands as a unique piece of Late-Career Leary History. This frame was discussed by Greg Mathias and Kyle A. Huffman in their Fireside BMX Chat podcast.
Additional information: The HLT frame series was associated with BMX pioneer Harry Leary, whose initials formed the “HLT” designation by Diamondback BMX.

Provenance/Significance: Official Racers Against Drugs (RAD) commemorative plaque awarded to Harry Leary. This piece served as a key authentication document, originating directly from Harry’s first personal storage locker. It was acquired alongside the 24" Diamondback Prototype, providing undeniable provenance for the bike’s factory origins and Harry’s personal ownership.
Additional information: Racers Against Drugs (RAD) was a BMX community initiative that promoted drug-free lifestyles through racing events and rider participation. Awards and plaques such as this one recognized riders like Harry Leary for their involvement in supporting positive youth messaging within the BMX community.

Item Name: 2012 UCI Worlds Winner Bike & Dual w1 -1st Place Felicia Stancil GT Speed Series
Provenance/Significance: The original GT Speed Series raced by Felicia Stancil to double gold at the 2012 UCI BMX World Championships in Birmingham, UK. This bike carried Felicia to 1st place in both the Junior Women’s Moto and the Junior Women’s Time Trial, earning her the coveted W1 (World #1) plate. Featuring the original race plate, this bike represents a dominant chapter in US Olympic-pathway history.
Additional information: The original GT Speed Series race bike used by Felicia Stancil to win double gold at the 2012 UCI BMX World Championships in Birmingham, United Kingdom. Stancil secured 1st place in both the Junior Women’s Moto and Junior Women’s Time Trial, earning the prestigious W1 (World Champion) plate. Retaining its original race plate, the bike represents a defining moment in American BMX’s Olympic development pathway and the early career of one of the sport’s most successful female racers.

Item Name:
First "Livermore" Boss - Boss Hogg Complete
Serial Number CR01
Provenance/Significance: The first 'Livermore' Boss Hogg ever produced. This complete Boss Hogg wears Serial Number CR01, marking the beginning of the brand’s manufacturing era in Livermore, California. As the very first unit off the line, this survivor represents the absolute origin of the Boss Hogg production run and is a cornerstone piece of American BMX manufacturing history. This frame was restored by C4 Labs and built frame up from there by Kyle A. Huffman.
Additional information: The first Boss Hogg produced following Boss Manufacturing’s move to Livermore, California, bearing Serial Number CR01. As the inaugural unit of the Livermore production run, this frame marks the beginning of a new manufacturing era for the brand. The frame was restored by C4 Labs and later assembled as a complete build by Kyle A. Huffman, preserving a foundational artifact of American BMX manufacturing history.

Item Name: Bill Allen's Signed Rad Movie Manuscript

Provenance/Significance: An original 6th Edition Rule Book from the National Bicycle Association (NBA). This document outlines the official regulations, track requirements, and rider classifications during a pivotal era of organized BMX racing. A vital piece of 'Paper Americana' that showcases the formalization of the sport's competitive standards.
Additional information: Original 6th Edition rule book from the National Bicycle Association (NBA), one of the primary sanctioning bodies of BMX racing during the sport’s formative competitive years. The publication details official race regulations, track standards, and rider classifications, illustrating the increasing organization and professionalization of BMX competition in the United States.

Provenance/Significance: An original JAG BMX factory recruitment flier and welcome letter, signed by Renny Roker, President of JAG BMX. This document outlines the 'JOX/JAG Team Package' and highlights the brand's unique focus on academic excellence, offering 'Honor Role' chevrons for riders. It name-drops legendary Factory Team members Brian Barlow, Paul Gossrau, and Jason Jensen, making it a key artifact of late 70s/early 80s racing culture.
Additional information: An original factory recruitment flier and welcome letter from JAG BMX, signed by company president Renny Roker. The document outlines the “JOX/JAG Team Package” and the brand’s distinctive emphasis on academic achievement, including the award of “Honor Roll” chevrons for student riders. It references early factory team members Brian Barlow, Paul Gossrau, and Jason Jensen, making it an important artifact of late-1970s to early-1980s BMX racing culture.

Provenance/Significance: An authentic piece of professional racing protection: Harry Leary's personal DIRTWERX helmet. As a companion piece to Leary’s helmet and race bikes, the jersey represents the functional design and visual style of the professional BMX circuit during the sport’s “Pro-Class” era.
